speaker: speaker_01
text: So what we do is we separate notes from any identical material and what we have is we have two sets of notes and for want of better words we call one set admin notes and one set clinical notes.

speaker: speaker_01
text: The admin notes should be your intake or your intake information, so the information about the client, their address, all that sort of stuff, all their personal details.

speaker: speaker_01
text: And that file is usually accessible to anybody within admin or anybody who needs to be able to contact the client for whatever reason.

speaker: speaker_01
text: But there is no personal, as in mental health, or clinical notes within that.

speaker: speaker_01
text: So there's nothing within that file that's going to breach confidentiality of your client to any admin staff or other staff who may need to access those notes for administrative purposes.

speaker: speaker_01
text: The other set of notes that you need are the clinical notes.

speaker: speaker_01
text: The clinical notes are your case notes that you take in sessions.

speaker: speaker_01
text: That should be in a separate file.

speaker: speaker_01
text: And there should be nothing within those notes that identifies who your client is or anything else that somebody could use that's going to breach confidentiality.

speaker: speaker_01
text: And that file should only be accessible to yourself.

speaker: speaker_01
text: If the file is going to be accessible to other people, you need to ensure that when you sign your counsellor client contract, that it is written within that contract whom those notes will be made available to.

speaker: speaker_01
text: So you might want to say the notes will be made available to your supervisor for supervision purposes.

speaker: speaker_01
text: Or if there's anybody else who may need to access those notes, that needs to be clearly stated within your counsellor client contract so that the client knows exactly who's going to have that information and who isn't.

speaker: speaker_00
text: CBT therapists use a particular session structure, which at first glance to somebody learning CBT might think is too structured.

speaker: speaker_00
text: But it's really a wonderful way to approach each session.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And I can explain why.

speaker: speaker_00
text: First of all, structure helps the therapist be more time efficient and helps the client begin to understand what they can expect from each session.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And generally, this is how our sessions flow.

speaker: speaker_00
text: First of all, when a client comes into the office, a therapist wants to maintain and reestablish that therapeutic alliance.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And so the first thing we do is we want to check on how our client's doing.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And in so doing, we're conducting a mood check.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We'll also look over the measures that a client filled out right before they saw us.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And if anything pops out from those, we can ask our clients right there and then about responses on the measures.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We also ask if there's anything that happened between last session and this session that's important for us to know about.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We refer to that as the bridge.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And that's also a way to reestablish that therapeutic alliance, sort of drawing the client's attention from last session to the current session.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then very important next step is to ask about the action plan or the experiment or the work they were going to do in between the two sessions.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And we do this for a couple of reasons.

speaker: speaker_00
text: First of all, clinicians understand that a lot of learning takes place between sessions.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We want the client to understand that we think it's important to check on that.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And if we don't ask about the action plan, the client might get the sense that it wasn't important if they did it or not.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So we always make sure to ask about the action plan.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then sometimes agenda items come right from that.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Sometimes clients were unable to do their between-session work.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then, of course, we want to know why.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Sometimes the client is very pleased about how they did their work between sessions, and that gets reviewed.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Next, the therapist asks what the client would like to work on.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We collaboratively set the agenda.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Very often, clients will bring up three, four, or more items for the agenda.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then the therapist would want to know, what's most important?

speaker: speaker_00
text: If we only have time to cover one or two items, what would that be?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And together, we decide what would be most important.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Sometimes clients don't know what to suggest for an agenda item.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And in that case, I might ask something like, well, is there anything you've got coming up that you could use my help with?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And we work on the agenda items, and then I'm certain to leave time.

speaker: speaker_00
text: for setting the action plan for the next week.

speaker: speaker_00
text: You don't want your clients to leave your session without knowing what they're going to work on, what they're going to experiment with, what they're going to try.

speaker: speaker_00
text: One of my favorite parts of the therapy session is asking the client for a summary, asking the client what he or she will remember this week, what's going to be most important for you to keep in mind this week, what was most helpful that we talked about.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And right then, I'm kind of helping the learning take place.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It's kind of like a moment where the client can think about what they learned and what's going to be most useful.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then I ask the client, and this is always a surprise to someone who's new to CPT, I always ask the client for some feedback about the session itself.

speaker: speaker_00
text: I say something like, how do you think this session went?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And is there something I could have done to make it a better session for you?

speaker: speaker_00
text: Is there something I could have done a little more of or a little less of?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And clients really, really appreciate this.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And therapists appreciate it, because we're always growing and learning as well.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So for example, one of my patients came in and he said,

speaker: speaker_00
text: Doctor, I'm so depressed, I think I want to kill myself.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And I said, why is that?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And he says, well, I'm just a total failure.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And I said, what way are you a total failure?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And he said, well, I'm a failure as a husband, as a parent, as a business person, as a homekeeper, as a home supporter, and so on.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So I could have, at that level, just talked about the sense of helplessness and failure.

speaker: speaker_00
text: But I wanted to get more information.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So instead of addressing helplessness, which seems to be across the board there, or the sense of failure, or the sense of frustration, I started taking each of these components in order.

speaker: speaker_00
text: keeping in mind that the overall meaning of what he said did have to do with failure.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It's pretty clear that his belief, I am a failure, had just grabbed hold of him and now was playing out in every aspect of his life.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So I asked, well, first of all, you've said your failure as a husband.

speaker: speaker_00
text: In one sentence, why and how do you think cognitive therapy works?

speaker: speaker_00
text: Well, first of all, I think it's a terrific question.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And the question is, what is cognitive therapy?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And so cognitive therapy is not just

speaker: speaker_00
text: a box of techniques.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It's not a toolbox anymore than, let's say, the Taj Mahal could be considered just a toolbox.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Cognitive therapy is actually an architecture.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And in order to understand cognitive therapy, you have to understand the blueprint that is provided the architecture.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Once you know the blueprint, then you can use the tools, and then you can build a building, or you can build a therapy based on that.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So the theory of cognitive therapy, very briefly, is that individuals in the course of their development

speaker: speaker_00
text: experience certain vulnerabilities.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And these vulnerabilities can be symbolized by maladaptive attitudes.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So one of the vulnerabilities that somebody might have is if I talk in public, people gonna ridicule me.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Or if I try something hard, I'm not gonna be able to make it.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And over a period of time, with some individuals,

speaker: speaker_00
text: These particular attitudes become fortified and kind of translocated.

speaker: speaker_00
text: They tend to permeate the individual's whole personality.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And so you get, in some cases, what you call personality disorder because these dysfunctional beliefs are so deeply encrusted that they kind of dominate the individual's interactions and adjustments.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So that's kind of what happens in the Axis II disorders.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And the Axis I disorders, particularly depression, which we've been seeing now, is that these negative attitudes that people have had over time become activated as a result of something that happens, a whole series of things that impinge on their specific vulnerabilities and their dysfunctional attitudes.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then as a result of that, they start to see things in a biased way.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So they go then from biased attitudes, such as I'm no good, to the biased perception, which is people don't listen to me, it means I'm no good.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And the perception itself then is twisted.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It's not based on evidence, but it's based on internal factors.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So how does cognitive therapy work?

speaker: speaker_00
text: Well cognitive therapy works by virtue of its changing certain key elements in this theoretical construction that I just gave you.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It works through changing the negative, in the case of depression, it works in changing the negative perceptions so that the person no longer sees things in terms of black and white, which is mainly black.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And more basically, it changes the biased beliefs such as I'm no good or I'm a failure or I'm unlovable and so on.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So when we do studies of the efficacy of cognitive therapy and we look for what accounts for the change, the improvement in cognitive therapy, we find that there's been a change in the basic attitudes and also in the perceptions.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So if you give people a test, we used to do this with a binocular test, and in a binocular test there are two images that are shown, one for each eye.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And when there are competing images coming into the eye, then certain psychological factors determine which of the two images you see.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It's something called binocular rivalry.

speaker: speaker_00
text: When people are depressed and you see two images, they're going to see the negative image.

speaker: speaker_00
text: When they get over the depression, they're going to see the positive image.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So that even at a perceptual level, cognitive therapy or any therapy that works is going to change not only the beliefs, but also the perceptions.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Now if you'd like, I could tell you the kind of pictures.

speaker: speaker_00
text: You interested?

speaker: speaker_00
text: Okay.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So one of the pictures we have, had when we did the study, is we had a table, there was a table, and there's a group of people sitting around the table, and there are several vases with flowers, and the people are all smiling and happy.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Then the companion picture,

speaker: speaker_00
text: is matched for the density.

speaker: speaker_00
text: However, around the table, on the table is a coffin.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And the same number of people are looking at the coffin instead of the flowers, and they all look very sad.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So the depressed person will see the sad picture.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Interesting, not by choice.

speaker: speaker_00
text: what happens automatically, sees the bad picture, but doesn't see the good one.

speaker: speaker_00
text: When they're treated successfully, the bad picture goes away and they only see the good one.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So this shows that at the most primitive level, people who are depressed see things negatively.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So how does cognitive therapy work?

speaker: speaker_00
text: It changes the negative image.

speaker: speaker_00
text: How does it change the negative image?

speaker: speaker_00
text: By getting at the basic negative beliefs, which is kind of a simplified way of putting it.

speaker: speaker_00
text: But I think that my way of thinking is the way it works.

text: And let's dive into what is CBT or cognitive behavioral therapy.

text: And in this case, what I mean by helpful is, you know, does it make us feel better and able to live our lives?

speaker: speaker_00
text: Or is it making our anxiety go so far up that it's interfering with our life?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And in this case, if it was giving us that 90% anxiety, I would say it's probably not a very helpful thought.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And keep in mind, there are some thoughts that are helpful that are not accurate.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So everything is great, might make you feel better, but might not actually be true.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Likewise, there are thoughts that are accurate that are really not helpful.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Maybe thinking a lot about how many people are sick is true and accurate, but if fixating on that is giving you anxiety, it might not be helpful.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So that's why it really has to be both.

speaker: speaker_00
text: In this case, because it didn't pass our helpful test, even though we don't have a final answer on accurate,

speaker: speaker_00
text: It didn't pass our smell test.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So we can move on to changing it.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So I brought back our cognitive mistakes, kind of our thinking bingo card, someone called it, and I thought that was great.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So we can kind of look over our thinking bingo card and see, hmm, is my thinking going kind of off the rails in any of these particular directions?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And we have other questions here I put in to ask yourself.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Am I overreacting?

speaker: speaker_00
text: Am I missing something?

speaker: speaker_00
text: What would I tell my best friend is one of my favorite questions when I'm changing a thought.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Usually we're nicer to other people than we are to ourselves and our own minds, so that's one that can really unlock a different way of thinking.

speaker: speaker_00
text: In this case, I think there's probably some prediction here since we're saying what we think is going to happen in the future.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And I think there's also some catastrophizing like kind of imagining the worst case scenario here so

speaker: speaker_00
text: Before we go and put our new thought in, I have one more caveat to add in.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And that is that our automatic negative thoughts, those ones that kind of pop up right away or maybe are going in our monologue all the time, are bumper stickers.

speaker: speaker_00
text: They're fast.

speaker: speaker_00
text: They're just a few words.

speaker: speaker_00
text: They're easy to remember.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Our new thoughts that we're working on that passed that smell test that are more balanced and helpful and accurate, they're more like Academy Award speeches.

speaker: speaker_00
text: They maybe go on and on.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We're going to be trading out like a few words for a few paragraphs, so it can feel a little bit daunting or awkward.

speaker: speaker_00
text: I just want to kind of name and normalize that.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It does get easier with time, but especially at the beginning, it can feel like

speaker: speaker_00
text: Oh, it just goes on and on.

speaker: speaker_00
text: But if we think about it, it makes sense.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Like if we're going to give a thought that's really accurate and helpful in painting a whole picture, it's probably going to be a bit more involved and have more words.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So let's take our example again.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Here we are.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Our things will never go back to normal thought.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And if we try and put a new thought in,

speaker: speaker_00
text: Here's what I came up with, and there are many, many right possible answers that work for different people.

speaker: speaker_00
text: But what I said was, no one knows exactly what will come next, and it probably doesn't help me to be predicting.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Things may not be exactly the same as they were before, but they also won't stay like this forever.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So it may be helpful for my anxiety to remember that even though it might not go exactly back to normal, it also, I'm not going to be stuck in my apartment totally alone forever.

speaker: speaker_00
text: That's not very likely to happen.

speaker: speaker_00
text: For other people, it might be helpful to think back to something else difficult that you went through and you might want to give a new thought that's about more like,

speaker: speaker_00
text: yes I'm going through a hard time and I don't know where the future and that's scary but I've been through other hard times and I know that I will get through it whatever the new normal ends up being.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So there's many ways to do this and your mileage may vary so it's helpful to practice and try what works for you.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then our final closing the loop here is once we have our new thought, we try it, and we ask ourselves, okay, I was 90% anxious when I thought things are never gonna go back to normal.

speaker: speaker_00
text: How anxious am I with this thought?

speaker: speaker_00
text: And the goal is not to get to zero, because remember, we still need a little bit of anxiety to spur us to wash our hands and be careful and thoughtful.

speaker: speaker_00
text: But if it goes down a little bit, that's a good sign.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So if we're making a move in that direction, that tells us we're on the right track.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So that's catch it, check it, change it.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Again, it takes some practice, but I don't think it takes too long to get good at this.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So hopefully you can give it a shot.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So let's switch gears now and talk a little bit about some behavioral techniques.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So we talked about thinking and how to catch and check and change our thoughts.

speaker: speaker_00
text: There's also ways that we can help change our behavior.

speaker: speaker_00
text: A lot of people have been exposed to different kinds of breathing exercises.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Sometimes they call it diaphragmatic breathing, abdominal breathing, relaxation breathing.

speaker: speaker_00
text: There's lots of names for it.

speaker: speaker_00
text: But I'm just going to teach one kind of simple technique and tell you why I think it's useful for acute anxiety.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Sometimes when we are in the moment and gripped by our anxious thoughts, we can't even grab our journal and try and change them because it's too overwhelming.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Our body might be in fight or flight mode.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So by starting with the breathing, it can actually

speaker: speaker_00
text: change our behavior, sometimes we hyperventilate a little bit when we're anxious and focusing on our breathing and slowing it down can get us back to a mental state where maybe we then can pull out the journal and do the work we just tried.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So if we sit kind of straight up with our shoulders back and our feet flat on the floor, it can help to put one hand on your chest and one hand like really low, can't see, really low down on your belly, kind of near your belly button or underneath it even.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And you can imagine a balloon inside your abdomen, inside your whole belly.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And when you breathe in through your nose slowly, you imagine that the balloon expands, go down.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And so the hand on your chest won't move.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Only the hand on your belly will go in and out as you expand and contract that balloon.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So if we take a deep inhale through our nose, and imagine the balloon expanding as you do that and inflating.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then when you breathe out through your nose,

speaker: speaker_00
text: the balloon deflates and shrinks down.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Doing that just a few times can interrupt the fight or flight process and can be really effective in reducing that anxious feeling.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So it can reduce your heart rate, reduce your blood pressure, and it's even been shown to lower the levels of cortisol in our body.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And cortisol is the stress hormone that goes up when we are experiencing either acute or chronic stress.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So the nice thing about this technique is you can do it anywhere.

speaker: speaker_00
text: You always have your lungs with you.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Nobody really knows what you're doing.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So you're not drawing attention to yourself if you're feeling a little shy about it.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And it can be really effective and pretty quick.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So really just taking a few deep slow breaths can reset everything.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So that's that.

speaker: speaker_00
text: I have one other behavioral technique to share which is less for

speaker: speaker_00
text: acute anxiety and more about like, how do we plan our behaviors and deal with the world right now and our lockdown situation and everything that's going on in a way that will still help us.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So this is an ACE log.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And I was thinking about this one because I'd come across this graphic on the internet, which I thought was so cute with the little fox and just talking about like, yeah, the things we can control and the things we can't and that feels so important to think about right now.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And our behavior generally is something that we can control,

speaker: speaker_00
text: But maybe we don't always know how or what things are going to be the best for us to change or to do more of.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So an ACE log, ACE stands for Achievement, Closeness, and Enjoyment.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And it's a really, really simple way of figuring out what behaviors are doing what for us.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So the way it works is you would just kind of write down day and time, what you did.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It can be very simple.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And then you're going to give a 0 through 10 score for your A, achievement, C, closeness, and E, enjoyment.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So let's say I clean my room on Saturday, and it's like a 9 for my achievement.

speaker: speaker_00
text: I felt pretty accomplished at the end of that.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Closeness, I did it by myself, so maybe I wasn't feeling very close.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And enjoyment, I'm glad it's done now.

speaker: speaker_00
text: I enjoy my clean room, but maybe I didn't enjoy it so much while I was doing it.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So that will kind of give you a picture.

speaker: speaker_00
text: The goal here is not that you get 10 out of 10 on everything, but it's more about noticing what your patterns are, what's working and not working.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So if you kind of just keep going and fill these things in, then you can look at it and say, oh,

speaker: speaker_00
text: wow, when I was reading the news for an hour on Monday, that was not doing anything for me.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Maybe I don't want to do that as much.

speaker: speaker_00
text: You can also look at patterns and say, wow, I'm getting a good amount of achievement and enjoyment.

speaker: speaker_00
text: I sleep running and cleaning my room and playing video games.

speaker: speaker_00
text: They all gave me

speaker: speaker_00
text: you know, some version of enjoyment or achievement, but my closeness column is kind of sad right now.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So that might give you an indication that it's time to do some activity that you think is going to give you a better read there.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So maybe the next day you're going to call grandma and that's going to give you some closeness points.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We all need all of these things.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We need to feel a sense of achievement.

speaker: speaker_00
text: We need to feel close to other people.

speaker: speaker_00
text: Even as we socially distance, we still need emotional closeness.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And we need to enjoy things in life.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So they may not be the same things that we used to do that gave us these achievement and closeness and enjoyment, but it's important that we spend some time thinking about what those new things are and how we can still have all of those needs met, even as the world around us is different.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So those are the behavioral techniques, our deep breathing and our ACE log.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So takeaways.

speaker: speaker_00
text: CBT is not magic.

speaker: speaker_00
text: I think it's really useful, and I hope you'll find parts of it useful, too, if you try some of it out.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And the truth is, the world is different than it used to be, and there's a lot of difficulty, and having emotions and reaction to that is totally valid and normal, and my job here is not to try and teach you how to erase your emotions or to, you know, make them stop.

speaker: speaker_00
text: This is about, again, when our emotions and particularly maybe our worry or anxiety are so big that they might be preventing us from having the kind of life we want to have, there are techniques that can give us more choices and more ability to feel okay again.

speaker: speaker_00
text: my recommendations are try stuff.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It's going to be different for every person.

speaker: speaker_00
text: It's not one size fits all solutions.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And what works for you might not work for your friend or your sibling or anybody else.

speaker: speaker_00
text: So figure out what it is that's good for you.

speaker: speaker_00
text: And I recommend doing that by taking that kind of experimental approach.

text: And good experimenters tend to write things down because it can be hard to remember the details and want to capture it as it's happening.

text: And if you do that, the ideal goal here is that you'll end up with some tools.
